title: Advanced RFID Security
subtitle: Tap, Scan, Secure — Build Your Own Access Control System!
overview: Metro cards, office key fobs, library cards, passport chips — RFID is everywhere in modern security systems. In this session, students go beyond basic RFID scanning and build an advanced access control system using Arduino and the MFRC522 RFID module. They implement multi-card whitelists, grant or deny access with LEDs and a buzzer, log entry attempts on an LCD, and explore real-world security concepts like UID spoofing and encryption.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — RFID reader wiring, card scanning demos, LCD output, student builds.

keyTopics: RFID — Radio Frequency Identification: uses radio waves to read data from tags and cards wirelessly
MFRC522 — The Arduino RFID reader module that operates at 13.56 MHz frequency
UID (Unique Identifier) — A unique code embedded in every RFID card/tag, like a fingerprint (e.g. A3 4F 2B 91)
SPI Protocol — Serial Peripheral Interface: the 5-wire communication used by MFRC522 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SDA, RST)
Whitelist — A list of approved UIDs stored in the Arduino code; only matching UIDs get access
Access Control Logic — Read UID → compare to whitelist → grant or deny → trigger LED + buzzer + LCD
Active vs Passive RFID Tags — Passive tags have no battery (powered by the reader's field); active tags have their own power
13.56 MHz vs 125 kHz — Two common RFID frequencies; MFRC522 uses 13.56 MHz (NFC-compatible, higher security)
UID Spoofing — A security vulnerability where attackers clone a card's UID to gain unauthorised access
Sector Authentication — Advanced MIFARE cards have 16 sectors with password protection beyond just the UID
Multi-Card Whitelist — Storing multiple approved UIDs in an array and checking each one with a loop
Entry Logging — Displaying the scan time, UID, and access result on the LCD or Serial Monitor for audit trails

activity.title: Build a Multi-Card RFID Access Control System
parts: RFID cards and key fobs (at least 2 per group)
Arduino IDE with MFRC522 and LiquidCrystal_I2C libraries
steps: Step 1 — Wire the MFRC522: Connect SDA → pin 10, SCK → pin 13, MOSI → pin 11, MISO → pin 12, RST → pin 9, 3.3V and GND
Step 2 — Read UIDs: Upload the DumpInfo example sketch, open Serial Monitor, and scan each card to record its unique UID
Step 3 — Build the Whitelist: Store 2–3 approved UIDs in a byte array in the code. Label them (e.g. 'Admin Card', 'Student Card')
Step 4 — Access Logic: Write a function that compares the scanned UID against the whitelist and returns true (match) or false (no match)
Step 5 — Visual Feedback: On match → green LED on + short beep + LCD shows 'ACCESS GRANTED'. No match → red LED on + long beep + LCD shows 'ACCESS DENIED'
Step 6 — Add Names: Map each UID to a name and display 'Welcome, Riya!' or 'Unknown Card!' on the LCD
Step 7 — Simulate an Intruder: Try scanning an unregistered card and confirm the denial system works correctly
Challenge: Add a counter that locks the system for 10 seconds after 3 failed attempts — just like a real security system!

options: Remote Frequency Input Device
Radio Frequency Identification
Rapid File Integration Data
Robotic Field Interface Design
quiz.explanation: RFID = Radio Frequency Identification! It uses radio waves to wirelessly read data stored in tags or cards — no physical contact needed. Metro cards, office fobs, and library cards all use RFID.

quiz.q: What is the name of the RFID reader module commonly used with Arduino?
options: HC-SR04
MFRC522
L298N
quiz.explanation: MFRC522 is the popular Arduino RFID module! It operates at 13.56 MHz and can read MIFARE cards and key fobs. It communicates with Arduino via the SPI protocol.

quiz.q: What is a UID in the context of RFID?
options: The power level of the RFID signal
A Unique Identifier — a fixed code embedded in every RFID card that distinguishes it from all others
The programming language used by the RFID chip
The distance at which an RFID card can be read
quiz.explanation: UID = Unique Identifier! Every RFID card has a unique UID (e.g. A3 4F 2B 91) — like a fingerprint. Your system reads this UID and checks it against the approved list to grant or deny access.

quiz.q: Which communication protocol does the MFRC522 use to talk to the Arduino?
options: I2C (SDA + SCL)
UART (TX + RX)
SPI (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SDA, RST)
Bluetooth
quiz.explanation: MFRC522 uses SPI — Serial Peripheral Interface! It needs 5 connections to Arduino: MOSI, MISO, SCK, SDA (SS), and RST. SPI is fast and reliable for short-distance communication between chips.

quiz.q: In an RFID access control system, what is a Whitelist?
options: A list of blocked or banned cards
A list of approved UIDs that are allowed to gain access
A log of all failed access attempts
A list of RFID frequencies
quiz.explanation: The whitelist contains the UIDs of approved cards. When a card is scanned, its UID is compared to the whitelist. Match = access granted. No match = access denied. Only cards on the list get through!

quiz.q: What frequency does the MFRC522 module operate at?
options: 125 kHz
2.4 GHz
433 MHz
quiz.explanation: MFRC522 operates at 13.56 MHz — the same frequency as NFC (Near Field Communication) used in contactless payments! This is different from older 125 kHz systems which have weaker security.

quiz.q: What is a Passive RFID tag?
options: An RFID tag with a built-in battery that broadcasts continuously
An RFID tag with no battery that gets its power from the reader's radio field
An RFID tag that only works in passive (standby) mode
An RFID tag that cannot be read wirelessly
quiz.explanation: Passive tags have NO battery! They harvest energy from the radio waves emitted by the reader. When you tap a metro card, the reader powers the card's chip wirelessly. Simple, cheap, and lasts forever!

options: Printing fake UIDs on a card's surface
Cloning a card's UID onto another card or device to trick the system into granting access
Changing a card's UID remotely via Bluetooth
Blocking an RFID reader with a signal jammer
quiz.explanation: UID Spoofing is a security attack! An attacker reads a valid card's UID using a hidden reader, clones it onto a blank card, and uses it to bypass a UID-only access system. This is why advanced systems use sector passwords too!

quiz.q: In the access control code, why do we store multiple UIDs in an ARRAY?
options: Arrays make the RFID reader scan faster
So we can loop through all approved UIDs and check each one against the scanned card efficiently
Arrays are required by the MFRC522 library
To encrypt the UIDs automatically
quiz.explanation: An array lets us store many approved UIDs and check them all with a single for-loop. Without an array, we'd need a separate if-statement for every card — messy and hard to update. Arrays make the whitelist scalable!

quiz.q: What does the system do when an UNREGISTERED card is scanned?
options: Automatically adds the card to the whitelist
Ignores the scan completely
Triggers the red LED and buzzer and displays ACCESS DENIED on the LCD
Resets the Arduino
quiz.explanation: Unregistered card = denied! The system lights the red LED, sounds a long beep on the buzzer, and displays 'ACCESS DENIED' on the LCD. The entry attempt can also be logged with the card's UID for security auditing.

quiz.q: What is Sector Authentication on a MIFARE RFID card?
options: A way to increase the reading range of the card
Password protection on specific memory sectors of the card, adding security beyond just the UID
A method of transmitting data wirelessly using sectors
Dividing the RFID signal into multiple frequencies
quiz.explanation: MIFARE cards have 16 memory sectors, each protected by a password (Key A and Key B). Reading or writing to a sector requires authentication. This means even if an attacker clones the UID, they can't access the card's stored data!

quiz.q: Which Arduino pin is connected to the MFRC522's RST pin, and what does RST do?
options: Pin 13 — it sends the clock signal
Pin 11 — it sends data to the module
Pin 9 — it resets the MFRC522 module, used to initialise and wake it up
Pin 12 — it receives data from the module
quiz.explanation: RST (Reset) is typically connected to pin 9. Pulling RST LOW resets the MFRC522 chip. The library uses this during setup to initialise the module. Without a proper RST connection, the module won't respond!

quiz.q: What is an Entry Log in a security system?
options: A wooden log used to block a door
A record of every access attempt — showing the UID, time, and whether access was granted or denied
A list of errors in the Arduino code
The buzzer sound pattern for granted access
quiz.explanation: Entry logging creates an audit trail! Every scan is recorded — who tapped (UID/name), when, and the result. Real security systems log this to a database or SD card so admins can review who entered and when.

quiz.q: A student adds a lockout feature — after 3 failed scans the system locks for 10 seconds. What programming concepts are needed?
options: Only a buzzer and LED
A counter variable to track failed attempts, a conditional to check if count reaches 3, and delay() or millis() for the lockout timer
A second Arduino and RFID module
The I2C protocol and an RTC module only
quiz.explanation: Great challenge! You need: a counter variable (int failCount = 0), an if-check (if failCount >= 3), and a timer (delay(10000) for 10 seconds). Then reset the counter. This mirrors real-world systems like ATM PIN lockouts!

quiz.q: How is an RFID-based access system different from a PIN-based system in terms of security?
options: RFID is always more secure than PIN
PIN is always more secure than RFID
Each has trade-offs — RFID cards can be lost or cloned; PINs can be forgotten or shoulder-surfed. Best security combines both (card + PIN = two-factor authentication)
They are identical in security level
quiz.explanation: No single method is perfect! RFID cards can be lost, stolen, or cloned. PINs can be forgotten or spied on. The most secure systems use two-factor authentication (2FA) — you need BOTH the card AND the PIN. Banks and high-security doors use this!
